C-84 - Reactors-Special Features of Aircraft Reactors
C-84 - Reactors-Special Features of Aircraft Reactors

This quarterly progress report of the Aircraft Nuclear Propulsion Project at ORNL records the technical progress of the research on circulating-fuel reactors and other ANP research at the Laboratory under its Contract W-7405-eng-26.
The ANP Project is comprised of about 530 technical and scientific personnel engaged in many phases of research directed toward the achievement of nuclear propulsion of aircraft.
The ART is to be a power plant system that will include a 60-Mw circulating-fuel reflector-moderated reactor and adequate means for heat disposal.

The loop power generation was estimated to have been 20 kw at the position of maximum insertion.
The power density was calculated to have been 0.6 kw per cubic centimeter of fuel mixture.

The alkali-metal-base mixture NaF-KF-LiF (11.5-42-46.5 mole %), with sufficient UF $_3$ and UF $_4$ added to give 11.9 wt %, was circulated in another Inconel forced-circulation loop, and, as previously, the attack was negligible, but there was a continuous metal deposit in the cold leg.
Mass-transferred deposits were found in the cold legs of loops operated with and without added oxygen, as $\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{O}_{2'}$ and with and without cold traps.

A loop operated with a maximum sodium temperature of $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ demonstrated the significant effect of temperature on mass transfer; the loop was free of deposited material, and there was no hot-leg attack.

A special thermal-convection loop was operated with thermocouples attached to the hot-leg wall, and it was found that a temperature difference of $160^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ existed between the maximum fluid temperature and the hottest section of the wall.

A series of six thermal-convection loops was operated with $\mathrm{NaF - ZrF_4 - UF_4}$ (50-46-4 mole %) to which various amounts of $\mathrm{ZrH_2}$ had been added to reduce the $\mathrm{UF_4}$ present to $\mathrm{UF_3}$ .

Two Inconel thermal-convection loops, 801 and 802, were tested with $\mathsf{NaF - ZrF_4 - UF_4}$ (50-46-4 mole %) under a nitrogen atmosphere to determine the possibility of substituting this cover gas for the currently used helium.

After operation for 500 hr at $1500^{\circ}\mathsf{F}$ , both loops showed light surface attack to a depth of 1 mil in all sections, with subsurface void formation to a depth of 10 to 13 mils in the hottest section.
The maximum attack in these loops slightly exceeded that for a standard Inconel loop with helium used as the cover gas; the attack in the standard loop is normally less than 11 mils.

The various sections from the condenser line that were examined metallographically exhibited intergranular cracking similar to that found in the loop operated for 400 hr.
In the hottest section of the condenser pipe, there was attack to a depth of 1 to 2 mils in the form of small subsurface voids.
